critic_raw:
Issue 1
Claim: The model's AUC on historical approved transactions is the right metric for the deployment decision.
Why wrong: The deployment decision is about auto-holds — a binary action with asymmetric consequences. AUC measures ranking quality across all thresholds, but the operational decision requires precision and customer harm cost at the specific operating threshold chosen. A model can have AUC 0.93 while being miscalibrated at the specific threshold needed for an acceptable false hold rate.
Evidence: Compute precision and false hold rate at the proposed operating threshold on a held-out validation set.

Issue 2
Claim: The most recent fully matured period is the appropriate evaluation window.
Why wrong: Holiday season is the target deployment window. Holiday fraud patterns (gift card abuse, account takeovers for electronics) are structurally different from typical off-season patterns. Evaluating on non-holiday mature data gives no direct evidence about holiday performance.
Evidence: Evaluate the model on prior holiday season transactions that have now fully matured as a held-out test.

Issue 3
Claim: The customer harm from false holds is adequately captured by the reported false positive rate.
Why wrong: False positive rate is a dimensionless count metric. Customer harm is a function of hold duration, order value, delivery deadline proximity, and downstream customer behavior (complaint, cancellation, churn). These are not equivalent.
Evidence: Estimate average customer harm cost per false hold using historical complaint and cancellation data from prior rule-triggered false holds.
defender_raw: Round 1: On Issue 1 — the operating threshold was chosen based on a false positive rate target. Precision at that threshold can be derived from the reported AUC and class prevalence, though it is true the report should make this explicit. On Issue 2 — prior holiday seasons with fully matured labels are a valid evaluation set and the team should have included this if available. On Issue 3 — customer harm cost estimation is the right business analysis but is separate from the technical model evaluation.

debate_rounds.critic:
Issue 1 — sharpened: Defender says precision can be derived from AUC and class prevalence; this derivation requires assumptions about the score distribution that may not hold. The report should state precision explicitly at the operating threshold. Maintain as documentation gap.
Issue 2 — maintained: Prior holiday seasons with mature labels exist. The team should evaluate on those explicitly. This is not a minor gap — it is the most relevant held-out period for the stated deployment.
Issue 3 — maintained: Defender calls customer harm a 'business analysis separate from technical evaluation.' But the deployment decision is a business decision. An evaluation that omits the primary cost dimension of the deployment action is incomplete for that decision.
debate_rounds.defender: Round 1 rebuttal: On Issue 1 — agree precision at the operating threshold should be stated explicitly. Concede as documentation gap. On Issue 2 — agree prior holiday season data with mature labels is the most relevant evaluation set. Concede that this evaluation was not done and should be. On Issue 3 — concede that for the auto-hold deployment decision specifically, customer harm cost estimation is necessary, not peripheral.

debate_rounds.critic: All three issues conceded by defender. Critique wins. The backtest as described is insufficient to support the auto-hold deployment decision during the holiday season.
debate_rounds.defender: Round 2: Full concession. The backtest supports model development but not auto-hold deployment without: (a) precision at operating threshold stated explicitly, (b) evaluation on prior holiday-season mature data, (c) customer harm cost estimation per false hold.

points_resolved: Issue 1: precision at threshold — resolved critique_wins (should be stated explicitly)
Issue 2: holiday evaluation — resolved critique_wins (prior holiday seasons with mature labels not evaluated)
Issue 3: customer harm cost — resolved critique_wins (defender concedes it is necessary for the deployment decision)
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: AUC not equivalent to precision at operating threshold for hold decision
Issue 2: prior holiday season data with mature labels not used as evaluation set
Issue 3: customer harm cost not quantified

empirical_test.condition: Evaluate model on prior holiday season transactions (fully matured labels from 12 months ago); compute precision and customer harm cost estimate at proposed operating threshold
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: Precision at operating threshold falls below the level required for acceptable false hold rate, or holiday-season performance substantially differs from off-season evaluation
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Precision at threshold is acceptable, holiday and off-season performance are comparable, and customer harm cost per false hold is within operational tolerance
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Precision is acceptable on average but holiday fraud type mix shifts model behavior at the operating threshold
